speaker: interviewer
text: With Elly Gross. Mrs. Gross, you'd say the situation in 1943 was not much different than '42, correct, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: '42 And '43 were about the same. Hard, tough, and looking every single penny. Not penny, was leu at the time and bani in Romania and Hungary-- well, in Hungary actually it was forint-- forint and filler. It was very hard. The life was very, very hard. And especially for my mother it was very hard because she had to make a living and keep the house. And in 1944, came the law-- actually, maybe in 19-- end of 1943, but I remember definitely in 1944. Jews were not allowed to travel. If somehow, somebody tried to travel, they were thrown off from the running train. Jews were beaten. Whoever--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. Just I heard. I never seen something like that. After dark no one was allowed to walk on the street. Not even in early morning. I still went to school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say, walk on the street, you mean you had to walk in the gutter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. You mean you just couldn't be outdoors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outdoors. You're not allowed to go out after dark and not early in the morning-- Jews. The were not allowed to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Curfew in other words.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a curfew. And, and we had to wear the yellow star. In the street, the kids whom I was playing years before, some of them, they were spitting after me. And they were saying that the star fall on your chest and something like that. It was very, very degrading and very hard. But the hard-- the really hard time came after. After my father was taken in 1942, we never heard of him again. Later I heard what happened, but I don't know if I should say now or later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on after the war, when I returned I thought I'm coming home to my parents. I heard that my father perished between 140 or 145 other young Jews from our city. They were next to Moscow. They decided to not coming back with the-- with the retrieving Hungarian and German army because they were just forced laborers. They hided there in an barrack in Morshanks, next to Moscow. And because they had no-- that was in January 1943-- and because they had no food, no heating, some of them frozen limbs-- had, had frozen limbs, they got sick. The Russians, they had no food for themselves, I don't know what happened exactly. They burned the barracks on themselves-- on them. And my father was burned alive, sick and with frozen limbs with the others, 140 or 145 from my town. And [SIGHS] we didn't know that, and I never heard it. Only after I returned from Auschwitz-- from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware that the Hungarians had sent a group of soldiers and forced laborers to help the Germans in their Russian campaign? Or you just knew he was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that he was taken, and I knew they're working-- making-- digging soil, cutting, chopping woods and something. I heard that way. We didn't know because no one came home for vacation. And no one was allowed to go there. The letters, they were censored if they wrote something. So nobody really knew what happened. Just they were working.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not aware of the Russian campaign of-- Hungary's so-called Russian campaign?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You just knew they were taken, and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the letters, as I said, were censored. They were not allowed to write, only that they are alive. From our father-- from my father I got-- we got only once a letter and never again. That was early in 1943. And because we never know exactly the date when my father was died, they was tolding me I should keep his memory as [INAUDIBLE] because it was in the spring of 1943. In 1944, as I mentioned before, we had to wore the yellow star. And because of the big curfew that we weren't allowed to walk on the street, we were just in constant worry. And we heard that they are going to take us away. We didn't know where, but I'm always blaming in my heart the Jewish leaders because they knew exactly what happening. Before the people-- like my father and other youngers-- they were taken away. They should make some kind of awareness for the people--

speaker: interviewer
text: A what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Awareness. You know, they should tell the people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, awareness.

speaker: interviewee
text: Awareness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of what was happening. And maybe they could of saved somebody. Maybe somebody could have run away to the other part of Romania which wasn't occupied by the Hungarians. They could have been saved or some children could have been saved somehow. But no one ever said nothing. And since the men, they were taken away, the mothers and the older ones, they never think about nothing and were just taking everything the way it was told. It was-- you know, they were able to take us with no effort, with nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You feel that the, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The leaders.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the important Jews of the town, were they taken away, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were taken also away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But--

speaker: interviewee
text: But you see, I'm not talking about the Jewish leaders exactly on the town. The Jewish leaders like they were in Budapest and the rabbi of Satmar, who actually was taken out from Romania-- I mean from Hungary-- and his followers were responsible. They knew because they ran away. They were taken away. They knew what happening, but they saved all their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean-- you mean Teitelbaum, the Satmar Hasidim?

speaker: interviewee
text: The leader-- I don't know his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I know he was taken out from Hungary. And he knew-- he knew if they taking him out, then it's something [BACKGROUND NOISE] happened. He didn't say nothing. He saved only his own skin. And you know, I'm not talking about the Jewish leaders in our town because they were just sacrificed lambs as we were. I'm talking about the big leaders as I feel now. At the time, I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a child. Please go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, right after Pesach, children from the school and especial the, the two of them, which I grew up and I played together, they became like [NON-ENGLISH] in the gymnasium. And they didn't have to learn already math or algebra. They had to learn just how to hit the Jew. That was the main-- the main subject in the schools because the little boys-- two, three years older than I am-- than I was at the time-- they came with-- they got rifles. And they came to the Jewish houses with the-- with their leader, and we were collected. We were forced to go out from the house. And the guy, whom I was-- the boy whom I was playing a few years before, he was there with his rifle on his back. And you know, it was so horrible that my mother wanted to take a little piece of bread, a little water, that we were taken out of the house-- a little jacket for my brother. And he said, dirty Jew. Go out. From my house, from my clothes-- I mean, from our clothes, from our food, They didn't let us to take nothing. We were taken out. We lived close to the-- to the-- to the center of the city on a nice, beautiful little street. And we were taken, as I said, with these youngsters and with their leaders. And while we were walking down on the street, they started almost at every Jew's house. Not almost-- every Jew's house. They knew exactly where the Jews lived. And they were forced out the same way as we were forced from the house. And we walked through the whole town. All day long we were walking because the line was growing. Finally-- we could have been there in two minutes or five minutes where my school was. But we were walking all day long with no food, no, no water, and some people they were really sick. They passed out. And they just-- I don't know exactly because I didn't see. Just I heard screaming. And I'm sure they just beat them or spit them. I don't know why they did with them because I didn't see it. Just I heard screaming. Finally, at the-- at late night we arrived to the school where I was attending. Next to the school was another yard. We called [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. She had over there some sheds, and we were placed in the sheds. But they told us, from the house we were not allowed to take not food, no nothing-- especially a knife, or a needle, or scissor. But some people they just took it. At night we were cold when we arrived to that [NON-ENGLISH] shed. The Hungarians leaders from the city, they said, everybody has to give in if has a ring, or earring, or a needle, or a knife, or something, somehow both with them to say the Hungarian government was safe. Who is not going to give in is going to be beaten. My mother didn't want to take a chance to keep it. She gave in her earrings, her wedding ring, a little chain. Also I had earrings and a little ring that I got a long time ago from my uncle. And we had a little knife that we kept with-- a very small knife. But the scissor we gave in and the needles. [SIGHS] And we slept on the floor. There was nothing above our head, only the star. On the next morning, nobody gave us any food. I don't know-- the children, they were crying for water, for food, for milk. I was thirty, too. But I don't know. Somehow I didn't say nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your little brother must have been upset.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very thirsty. But my mother had no water to give him. We were sitting and putting our head on top of each other on a small little spot on the floor. The star was above us. Luckily, it wasn't raining. The next morning, we started to walk all the way out from our town for, I don't know-- in a row. I don't know. It was five, or three, or four, or whatever. I'm not sure. I don't remember that. We had to walk out to [HUNGARIAN], it was called in Hungarian. And in Romanian, [ROMANIAN], a little, little, very small town that had a big factory. We walked over there about five kilometers. And when we walked over there, we were put in shed. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And on the shed also was no-- a partial roof was. It was we're sleeping, put on the floor. We had nothing. And when the rain-- later on, it was raining-- we're staying on the mud. When the sun was shining, the sun--

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people were there, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole town.

speaker: interviewer
text: About?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole city. I don't know. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: A couple of hundred, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of hundreds. Maybe more than a thousand. Maybe around a thousand because not only the city people, they were both over there. They were both from the surrounding towns-- Jews. I don't know. I don't know. It was a small town. Maybe-- I can't remember. I don't know because I never knew that, never asked. Never even asked how many were in there-- very crowded, was no food. Finally, the next day I think they brought from the city big buckets. And they made in the ground a hole and there were boiled potatoes. And it was given to us. The people who came from the towns, they had food they brought with themselves. So they have relatives in the city, they have the relatives offered food. Then I heard that the Jews-- especially older, because only a few older man was already over there with us-- they were taken and beaten to give out if they had any valuables-- gold or money hidden in their home. Because some people, they were well off. And one of my mother's friend's husband also was beaten. And he previously was in the United States and he brought something with him from here and when he returned to Romania. And he was beaten very badly. Broken bones, I heard. I was at the time in 1944 15 years old. I was-- they put me to clean potato in that made kitchen. Was no kitchen. It was outside. And I was very happy when I was able to put a little potato peel in my mouth, washed potato peel to eat. We were there about four weeks in that misery. And I was thinking all the time how the sky is so beautiful blue and the sun was shining. And nothing didn't became dark. [CRYING] After four weeks, we heard that they are going to take us away. With my mother and brother we were between the first group. They made a little tent, and we had to walk across the water. The water Crisana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that a river?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very small little river.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the tent they were checking us that we shouldn't have food, or knife, or gold, or whatever, money with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say, checking, would they search you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They strip checked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Strip searched?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strip searched, exactly. And then we had with us about eight boiled potato. Somehow they let us take the eight boiled potato. We were put in the cattle wagons about 90 to 100 person. It was so tight that we had no room to sit. But between the legs to each other, we were sitting that way, the three of us. And the knife-- somehow I was able the little knife to take in into wagon. I don't know how they'd let in my hand. Maybe my hand was closed and somehow they didn't take away from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a pocket knife?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very small, little pocket knife-- little, very little, like this small. So with the little knife I made a little hole. I started to make a hole in the wagon because we had no air while we were sitting down. So there we got a little air in for the three of us. We had no food. [SIGHS] And it was put there two buckets-- one with water, one for, you know, if somebody had to go to the toilet. That was a Saturday. Exactly the same day was coming, the holidays as we have it now. It was erev Shavous on the Saturday. On that night, we were taken-- already closed the wagon-- we were taken back to the city which was the first day of Shavous on Sunday-- Sunday early morning. Some of the Christians, they came to see us. Some of them, they brought food to people. And they give-- they somehow they let to give in on the window, you know, that cattle wagon windows. We had no one to bring us food, but we had the eight boiled potato. And on that about noon, the train started with us. We were taken about three days. And then after three days the train halted. And they opened the wagon and they said, from every wagon, one person could go out to change the water, to bring water and change the bucket that was used. And nobody wanted to go out. So my mother told me, I should go. I was 15. I felt a grownup. I went. The water was a river there. It was so-- it was after rain. The water wasn't clean but everybody was very happy to get the water. So everybody drink. The train started again. Somebody died. A woman gave birth to a child. We arrived to on the Friday, on the following Friday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how many days was this all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Sunday, six days. Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Friday-- six days. We arrived Friday early morning to Auschwitz. Since I made that little hole, I was able to see, look out on the close to the floor. And I saw-- the first thing I saw, a big, big sign-- 'arbeit macht frei.' I learned a little German in the school. And I was able to read it. It was a big, big fence, a big gate. And I heard a nice music. Then after they opened the door, and a man with a striped clothes came in, [SPEAKING GERMAN], and screaming. They took away the children from the younger mothers. And they throw the children. It was everybody screaming and yelling. I was so-- I didn't even-- since we didn't talk so much in that six days when we were traveling, I was [? sort ?] of kind of numb. I didn't even know how to open my mouth. And my mother-- since my younger brother-- my brother was five years old. I was 15. She said to me, because they said-- they going-- they-- they screamed-- they are going to take the children. We saw they were taking away from the mothers. My mother said, she's going to give my brother to one of the neighbors who had three small children. And I said to my mother, no because I'm older and my brother is younger. And I could take better care of myself. At that moment, I killed my mother. [SOBBING] [INAUDIBLE] I am getting all my life. Because at that moment if I don't tell my mother to go with my brother, she would have remained at least alive, alive with me. In my life whenever I had hardship, always I say, if I killed my mother, I deserve it, [CRYING] all the punishment. [SIGHS] My mother went to the left with my brother. I went to the right. I waved to my mother, and I've never seen her again and my brother. [CRYING] We were taken-- we were put in a row of five. We were taking to a big room. I remember the room. And I seen again a few years ago when I visited Auschwitz-- a big, big hole with benches around and hooks on the wall. And it was telling us everybody should tie the shoe, and put nice little clothes on, and remember the number of the hook that you should know where to pick up your clothes when you finish the shower. [SIGHS] I was somehow slow because I'd never been without my mother. And I missed her already. And I saw that I would have been the last ones who walked out from that room, that the clothing, like it was just slipping together. Nobody kept any more on the hook or on the bench.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards they were Germans. And they were standing there next to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men or women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mens. Mens, womens, mixed. And we had to take off everything from us. In the next room-- I had beautiful long ponytails, blond. It was cut off my hair and shaved my head. Then we were-- then we were ushered in another room where was shower. And suddenly came hot water on us then after cold water. Just they were laughing that cold and hot water one after other changed every second. When we walked out from there, I-- I got a wood-- that Holland type of wood shoe. I don't know how they call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clog. And a long dress with a yellow cross on the back. My shoe-- I never-- I didn't get the shoe back that I had. From there we walked for a while. This was-- as now after I visited Auschwitz, I know exactly where we were the first time. The first time we were in Auschwitz One and were walking to Birkenau. And in Birkenau, we were only 2 and 1/2 kilometers one from each other. We were there on the early morning around the noon on Friday. It was a rainy day. It very cold and I was shivering. And that dress and all this-- the eight potatoes what we had on the whole rows with my mother, I'm sure she gave us most of it for the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [SIGHS] I didn't feel hunger. I just started to cry after my mother. And came over there. We were taken to Lager C. I know now where I was taken. And I knew after it, too. But at the time I didn't know. At the plaza, we were standing over there in row of fives. And I started to cry, where is my mother? And somebody said rudely, did you see that smoke? We saw some chimneys around us, about five. Not close to us, but you could see the sky was heavy smoke. And as I said, it was raining, drizzling-- not really raining, drizzling. It was very cold. And you see that heavy smoke there? They going on. I started to holler, scream, and cry, hysterical screaming. Who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman, you know, who was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Another prisoner or a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could have been a prisoner. It could have been a German. I didn't-- I don't know exactly who it was. She wasn't wearing-- she was wearing civil clothes. And well she had to be Czechoslovakian because she spoke Hungarian. She said Hungarian because she answered in Hungarian. I'm just repeating-- I mean correcting myself. And she saw me so hysterical and getting. She heard me crying. She said, don't worry. On Sunday, there is going to be a family reunion for the families. Oh, I was so naive. I believed her. And I was all the time waiting for that family reunion because I was hoping that's the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: From there we were taken to the Barrack 20 of the Lager C. And the Barrack 20 wasn't finished. What was it? Since it was raining, was water on the floor. And we had no food for so long. We were very hungry. We were very tired. And all this commotion what went on in this last week with us. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When it was given us food for the first time in a big, big bucket, everybody was allowed to swallow three times. You'd took to the, like-- everybody, one after other. The other person was watching that the first one shouldn't swallow more. But they were really cautious. I didn't swallow more than three times. And the next day-- all night it was raining. Water was just pouring on us. We were standing in the water until the knee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until the what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until knee height.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Water. And at night, I heard children screaming, you know, because on that road where we were walking from Auschwitz to Birkenau, other groups, they were walking, too. And I don't know. Somehow I wasn't even sleeping because we couldn't sleep. We were just standing in the water. The next day-- the next day, every block-- there were 32 blocks in Lager C. Every blockalteste in every block had to increase their inhabitants because the Number 20 block where I was was not for to stay. Because there was water, as I said. There was no place to stay the people. And for the blockalteste-- the Block 18, which was the blockalteste Laichman, Miri-- Miri Laichman, that's the, the rabbi's daughter from Bratislava-- she said-- she came to our-- she said on her block that if anyone has relatives from the [NON-ENGLISH] from where I was-- where I am, they should come because she has to increase her people with 100 or 150. That was told to ever single barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the Barrack 20 was my two cousins, Eva and Yboia Farkas from Marghita. And they said, they have in [NON-ENGLISH] the aunt, my mother Irenka-- Irina-- and Ellie, their cousins. And they came over and they took me from there. So I was taken from the-- from with my townspeople to the other ones which were from Bihor, from Marghita. From the other town.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the other town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. We'll continue.
